title: 3GL Support on Oracle

3GL Support on Oracle

3GL Support on Oracle

The U6.x Oracle database connector provides 3GL service functions for use by 3GL routines that access Oracle.

Pro*C/C++ versus OCI

It is possible to use the Pro*C/C++ precompiler interface, or the Oracle Call Interface (OCI), or both in the same application.

Same Logon Path vs. Independent Logon

Your 3GL functions can access Oracle either on the same logon path as the ORA connector, or on an independent logon path (Oracle connection):

  • When you access Oracle on the same logon path as your ORA connector, the database operations performed by your 3GL routines are in the same transaction as those of the ORA connector.
  • When you open an independent concurrent connection, the database operations performed by user-defined 3GL and those of the ORA connector are in two independent transactions.

First Logon Path

When you use your own 3GL routines with Oracle, you must keep in mind the distinction between the first logon path to Oracle, and subsequent concurrent logon. The first logon path is defined as either one of the following two possibilities:

  • The first path opened after the application starts
  • The first logon path to Oracle opened after the previous first logon path was closed

Note that it is possible to have a situation where there is no first logon path open. For example, consider the following sequence of events:

  • A logon path to Oracle is opened at 4GL level. This is the first logon path.
  • A second concurrent logon path to Oracle is opened via 4GL or 3GL.
  • The first logon path is closed at the 4GL level. This means there is no longer a firs t logon path, even though a logon path is still open.
  • A third logon path to Oracle is opened. This is now considered the first logon path.

Only the first logon path of the ORA connector is accessible by user-defined 3GL.

CAUTION: Do not attempt to access the connection when the first logon path no longer exists.

Sharing a Connection with the Oracle Connector

When your 3GL routines access Oracle on the same connection as the ORA connector, you must meet the following requirements:

  • Do not disconnect from Oracle in your 3GL routines; instead, close the logon path to Oracle at the 4GL level.
  • Do not commit or rollback in user-defined 3GL; instead, commit and rollback on the logon path at the 4GL level.
  • Do not exceed the maximum number of cursors allowed by the Oracle server. This maximum is determined by adding the following two factors:
    • The number of cursors opened by the ORA connector
    • The number of cursors opened by user-defined 3GL

    You might want to increase the OPEN_CURSORS initialization parameter of Oracle. The maximum number of cursors opened by the U6.x ORA connector is controlled by the open cursors connector option.

  • In most cases, user-defined 3GL can access the connection only after the ORA connector has successfully created the first logon path to Oracle. The technique for the ORA connector requires that your 3GL routine creates the connection before the ORA connector logs on. In both cases, coordination of the order of events at the 3GL level and at the 4GL level may be required.
  • Your 3GL routines are responsible for closing the Oracle cursors they have opened before the logon path is closed.

Using the U6.x ORA Connector

By default, the U6.x connector creates the first logon path to Oracle using the Pro*C/C++ precompiler interface. The first logon path is associated with the default connection; that is, it is created with a CONNECT statement without an AT clause.

Accessing the Connector's Connection with the Pro*C/C++ Precompiler

To access Oracle on the same connection as the ORA connector using the Pro*C/C++ precompiler interface, your 3GL routines can access the first logon path of the ORA connector simply by executing SQL statements without an AT clause.

Do not set the ORA connector option disable precompiler connect, since this makes it impossible to access Oracle on the same connection as the ORA connector using the precompiler interface.

Accessing the Connector's Connection with the OCI Interface

To access the connector's connection to Oracle, your 3GL routine must obtain the address of the LDA of the connection. You can use the 3GL service functions URETURNLDA and UGETUOPENFLAG for this purpose.

Furthermore, an application can use UGETUSVCCTX to get the Oracle Context, and Environment handles. Prototypes are OCISvcCTx*UGETUSVCCTX(), OCIEnv*UGETUENV().

Accessing an Independent Connection with the Pro*C/C++ Precompiler

User-defined 3GL can create either the precompiler default connection, or a non-default connection. The default connection allows you to issue embedded SQL without explicitly identifying a connection.

To create the precompiler default connection, set the ORA connector option disable precompiler connect with the assignment setting USYS$ORA_PARAMS. If you do not set this option, the ORA connector creates its first logon path with the precompiler interface, and uses the default connection for this purpose. This can cause obscure Oracle errors to occur either in your 3GL routines or in the ORA connector, since it cannot create more than one default connection.

A default connection is made by a CONNECT statement that has no AT clause. For example:

EXEC SQL CONNECT :UserPassword USING :Database;

The USING clause is optional. See your Oracle precompiler documentation for more information.

Alternatively, your 3GL can make one or more non-default connections using a CONNECT statement with an AT clause. Use an AT clause with SQL statements which must be executed on this connection. When your 3GL routine makes a non-default connection, you do not need to set the connector option disable precompiler connect. For more information on the use of the AT clause, consult the Oracle precompiler documentation .

Accessing an Independent Connection with the OCI Interface

For more information on setting up an OCI connection, consult your Oracle documentation.
